data.title: Voya Target Solution Trust Series Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

These portfolios are only offered as an investment option within variable products and retirement programs.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the Fed’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 bp, ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the Fed to continue its gradual easing.

Supported by this positive outlook and President Trump’s pro-business proposals, we continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks. Large-cap valuations appear expensive, but they are supported by strong corporate profitability. The increasing weight of the technology sector with its capital-light business models suggest that higher margins may be the new norm, making expectations more reasonable. With the election concluded, corporate spending is expected to resume and drive positive earnings growth as well. Mid-caps offer enticing valuations and are poised to benefit from a broadening expansion and lower borrowing costs and provide more financial stability than small caps.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more enactment of monetary stimulus and its commitment to future fiscal measures indicate that policymakers are prepared to take action to meet their economic targets, which could provide a tailwind for emerging market stocks. In December, U.S. large-cap equities were reduced, and short-term bonds were increased. This move came after the post-election run-up and the elevated valuations, as we deemed it prudent to lock in some gains and diversify away from the momentum trade. In terms of manager changes, the BlackRock MSCI EM Index CIT was added to implement the tactical increase in EM equities.

In fixed income, U.S. IG core bonds offer decent yields and portfolio stability. While conviction on rates near-term direction is limited, we believe core bonds will hedge against equity risk due to the transition from inflation to growth risk that has occurred. While credit stress and defaults are expected to remain controlled, high yield spreads are very tight, leading us to prefer equities for risk allocation. In addition, an expectation that the U.S. dollar rally will continue and a positive U.S. economic outlook justify a preference for U.S. assets and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

Positioning

At the beginning of the period, Trusts were tactically overweight to U.S. mid-cap stocks and underweight in U.S. large-cap stocks. Emerging market (EM) equities were increased to remove an underweight by selling U.S. large cap and small cap. China’s recent enactment of monetary stimulus and its commitment to future fiscal measures indicate that policymakers are prepared to take action to meet their economic targets, which could provide a tailwind for emerging market stocks. In December, U.S. large-cap equities were reduced, and short-term bonds were increased. This move came after the post-election run-up and the elevated valuations, as we deemed it prudent to lock in some gains and diversify away from the momentum trade. In terms of manager changes, the BlackRock MSCI EM Index CIT was added to implement the tactical increase in EM equities.

Overall, Trusts continue to maintain a balanced posture with a preference for U.S. large and mid-cap equities, and core IG fixed income.

Performance

The Voya Target Solution Trusts’ primary performance objective is to outperform its strategic allocation composite benchmark over the long-term through tactical asset allocation, i.e., deviating from the composite benchmark over the short and medium-term and active manager selection. The benchmark return is the weighted average return of indices that represent asset classes included in the strategic allocation benchmark. Index returns are gross-of-all fees. The Trusts are generally rebalanced monthly and the strategic asset allocations are updated annually to reflect changes to our capital market assumptions. In the fourth quarter of 2024, Trusts’ outperformed their strategic allocation benchmarks. Tactical asset allocation was a detractor, but manager selection contributed.

Tactical asset allocation had a negative impact on performance during the period. Trusts’ tactical underweights in U.S. large cap and overweights to EM equities was the main detractor, as large U.S. stocks delivered another period of positive performance, helped by continued strong corporate earnings and despite sharply rising long-term interest rates. EM equities struggled due to geopolitical tensions, economic uncertainties in key regions and a stronger U.S. dollar.

Underlying managers’ relative results were positive across the Trusts. Strategies that contributed most to excess returns in the quarter were Voya Mid Cap Growth, Voya Large Cap Value and Wellington International Opportunities. The biggest detractors in the quarter were Voya VACS Series MCV, MFS Growth Equity and Voya VACS Series EME.

You should consider the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses of the variable product and its underlying fund options; or mutual funds offered through a retirement plan, carefully before investing. The prospectuses / prospectus summaries / information booklets contain this and other information, which can be obtained by contacting your local representative or by calling (800) 992-0180. Please read the information carefully before investing.

Disclaimer

Past performance is no guarantee of future returns. All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. All security transactions involve substantial risk of loss. Please reference your client statement for a complete review of recent transactions and performance.

This commentary has been prepared by Voya Investment Management for informational purposes. Nothing contained herein should be construed as (i) an otter to sell or solicitation of an otter to buy any security or (ii) a recommendation as to the advisability of investing in, purchasing or selling any security. Any opinions expressed herein reflect our judgment and are subject to change. Certain of the statements contained herein are statements of future expectations and other forward-looking statements that are based on management’s current views and assumptions and involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results, performance or events to ditter materially from those expressed or implied in such statements. Actual results, performance or events may ditter materially from those in such statements due to, without limitation, (1) general economic conditions, (2) performance of financial markets, (3) interest rate levels, (4) increasing levels of loan defaults (5) changes in laws and regulations and (6) changes in the policies of governments and/or regulatory authorities.

The opinions, views and information expressed in this commentary regarding holdings are subject to change without notice. The information provided regarding holdings is not a recommendation to buy or sell any security. Fund holdings are fluid and are subject to daily change based on market conditions and other factors.

©2024 Voya Investments Distributor, LLC • 230 Park Ave, New York, NY 10169 • All rights reserved.

data.title: VLU: There Are Better Options To Find S&P 1500 Exposure
data.description:
What is VLU?

The SPDR S&P 1500 Value Tilt ETF (NYSEARCA:VLU) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as ETF) that invests in American listed stocks representing the S&P 1500, a mix of large, mid, and small-cap stocks. The point of this ETF is to have a cheap, passively managed option to track outperforming stocks within the S&P 1500 index, which tracks almost the full U.S. index, with the fund manager rebalancing and reviewing the fund quarterly or less for investment opportunities.

VLU's benchmark target is the S&P 1500 Low Valuation Tilt Index, and this article will tell you whether it's worth investing with VLU or another asset manager/fund when thinking about investing in a U.S. market index. This Index adjusts the weighting approach of the S&P Composite 1500 Index, overweighting stocks with relatively low/cheap valuations and underweighting those with higher/richer valuations. It is to be noted that the S&P Composite 1500 Index, a relatively key benchmark for the U.S. equity market, is a market-cap-weighted blend of the large-cap S&P 500 Index, the S&P MidCap 400 Index, and the S&P Small Cap 600 Index.

Passive investing ETF's mean that the fund managers don't make many trades, and only rebalance every once in a while, versus active managers, who have the mandate to buy and sell stocks more often if they feel that there's a compelling investment opportunity. I've worked in capital markets credit risk in the fund business at one of the largest banks in Canada, and have found that more often than not, these actively managed ETFs are simply not worth the high fees.

After further review, my analysis shows that VLU is the best investment vs. others passively managed peers. The fund's benchmark has underperformed others passively managed and low fee S&P 1500 tracking ETF's over the long term with higher fees. I recommend investors buy the SPDR Portfolio S&P 1500 Composite Stock Market ETF (SPTM) if investors want passively managed, low fee options for this benchmark with higher returns.

Fund Breakdown

VLU was launched on October 24, 2012, by State Street Global advisors with a mandate to seek to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the S&P 1500 Low Valuation Tilt Index. The fund overweights stocks with relatively low valuations and underweights stocks with relatively high valuations, noting that the managers seek "stocks that exhibit the strongest value characteristics based on: price to book ratio, price to earnings ratio, price to cash flow ratio, price to sales ratio, and dividends paid". Unfortunately though, if you look at the returns on many of these funds that try to passively trim the fat, so to speak, there isn't always a link to see the risk vs. reward returns that are mentioned in the prospectus strategy as the target.

That aside, the fund has been trading for years, with a NAV of $183MM and a daily volume of about 10,000 shares traded. The idea of the fund isn't bad - essentially, the managers are looking for undervalued stocks within the index to drive outperformance and yield. But upon further review of the ETF's results, I'm skeptical that the fund has fully achieved what it set out to do. Below is a chart over the past 3 years and 10 years, showing returns slightly below other passively managed peers with lower fee structures.

The fund also employs a 0.12% yearly ETF expense ratio for fees to the asset manager (as of Dec. 31/24), which is cheap, but still 4x higher than SPTM (0.03%). Ultimately, the fund is decent, but I don't think it's the best option based on the previous results and history.

Previous Results and Fund Portfolio

When comparing VLU's portfolio to peers, there are subtle differences in the portfolio breakdown, but not many. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the portfolio mostly resembles the S&P 1500, with a lower weighting in technology, and slightly higher weights in financials and consumer staples. Overall though, this fund is marginally different from the benchmark and its similar, though a bit lagging, performance proves it.

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.94 notes that the fund will have less volatility than the benchmark, which makes sense for a value fund. That has borne out in past results with lower lows and some, but never long-lasting, bounce backs. Additionally, when evaluating VLU's risk-adjusted returns, I analyzed the Sharpe ratio to see whether the fund has generated excess returns over the risk-free rate when adjusted for total volatility. Unfortunately, the Sharpe ratio for the fund is 0.28, well below the score of 1+ to prove strong risk-adjusted returns. And from a macroeconomic perspective, small-cap growth stocks are more sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. With the Federal Reserve taking a wait and see approach to rate cuts given upcoming tariffs, mid and small-cap stocks may be left in limbo, after the last few years of underperformance due to higher borrowing costs. Given VLU's underperformance vs. peers, I'd rather buy SPTM if I expect a rally in the total U.S. market to occur.

Risks and Bottom Line

The bottom line for this fund, in my opinion, is that the juice is not worth the squeeze. There's simply not enough good news over the past 10 years to justify this fund strategy, and investors can simply get normal S&P 1500 exposure through SPTM. VLU doesn't have enough differentiation and hasn't outperformed to convince me it's a better ETF alternative than other passive counterparts. It's also less liquid, with only ~$1.85MM of daily volume, compared to ~$43MM for SPTM. With Trump announcing tariffs and the market already looking shaky, I'm already weary of a full 100% equity portfolio, and if you're thinking about a total U.S. market index, I don't think VLU is the best option of the bunch.

data.title: ECH: Well Positioned For 2026 And Beyond
data.description:
Opportunity Overview

Chile's stock market has been through a lot of turbulence since 2019. Political tensions elevated in 2019, and Covid economic shocks briefly sent the economy into a recession in 2023.

However, the market has had a strong start to 2025 and has finally caught up to emerging markets after months of underperformance.

Even after bouncing back in the past 6 months, the iShares MSCI Chile ETF (BATS:ECH) trades at a slight discount to MSCI Emerging markets. It also trades at a strong discount to its historical average. The market may rerate Chile in 2026 once the election passes and there is more clarity about global economic conditions.

Chile looks positioned for a long term bull market after it goes through some of the challenges of 2025. Trade war tensions will be very strong this month, likely resulting in a substantial pullback in emerging market equities. Chile will also be holding its elections in November this year, which may add to this volatility.

Chile's economy has improved substantially, as seen by its Q4 performance. The market looks like an absolute bargain relative to other emerging market equities, and 2025 still looks like a solid entry point, especially if there are strong pullbacks this month.

The iShares MSCI Chile ETF is a solid vehicle for Chile's stock market, with strong exposure to banking and materials stocks. This ETF looks positioned to continue outperforming emerging market equities in 2025 and 2026. Any potential dips, caused by the trade war or domestic political concerns, would create an even better entry point. I think the pros of the long-term potential and relative value here outweigh the short-term risks Chile will experience this year.

Economic Outlook

Chile's economy looks like it is heading in a positive direction in 2025, which should help support equity market sentiment. Chile's economy has quickly bounced back from the 2023 recession, and there will be more political clarity after the country's elections later this year.

Recent Growth Picks Up: Chile's economy had much slower growth at the start of 2024, resulting in a pullback in the equity markets. However, the country's Q4 2024 GDP growth was 4%, approaching the highs of 2022.

Chile's GDP Growth

This is an encouraging sign, although growth will be a bit slower in 2025 and 2026. The World Bank is calling for 2.2% growth in Chile in 2025–2026. This growth will be slightly lower than that of peers like Argentina, Colombia, Peru, and Brazil.

Chile mainly exports to China and the United States, so trade war tensions will have a strong impact on its growth potential. On a positive note, Chile's main export is copper, and the price movement of copper will likely be very favorable this decade due to new sources of demand from EVs/clean tech and also because of supply concerns.

Additional rate cuts could also help support further growth. Chile's Central Bank has still been holding rates steady and may be several quarters away from rate cuts. Chile's Central bank hasn't cut rates since December 2024, when it cut rates by 25 bps.

At the moment, Chile's Central Bank is currently targeting a 3% inflation rate in 2025–2026 and will need to see several months of stable inflation before cutting rates again.

Inflation peaked at around 14% in 2022 and has since improved substantially, although it has risen slightly since the beginning of 2024.

Chile Inflation Rate

Mining Investments: The growth of Chile's mining industry should support economic growth, as the government plans to invest over $80 billion in new mining projects through 2033. Around 90% of new mining investments have targeted copper mining.

The demand for copper should grow in the lower single digits this year, which could result in a supply deficit in 2025. In the long run, the demand for copper will be strongly driven by traditional sources, as well as new sources like electric vehicles and clean technology.

This is one long-term catalyst that makes it worthwhile to ignore some of the short term challenges this year, especially since the market still appears priced for these political and trade war shocks.

Public Debt: Many emerging markets had to take on records amount of debt following Covid, which put a lot of strain on these economies. Public debt in emerging markets has risen by around 8 percentage points since 2019.

Chile's government has been no exception, but it is currently in a much better position because of the low base effect.

ETF and Equity Markets

The iShares Chile ETF only invests in 25 companies and has a slightly higher management fee of 0.59%. The yield is not that attractive, but there are better options available if you target ADRs in specific sectors like banking.

Valuation thoughts: One intriguing fact about this ETF is that it trades at a substantial discount to MSCI Emerging Markets. Historically, Chile's valuation has been much higher, and it appears that the market is beginning to turn the corner following its brief recession and political concerns during 2022–2023.

Chile's Historical P/E Ratio

Trends have been positive in 2025, and the market's PE ratio will likely be able to expand in 2026 after the trade war risks and the November election passes.

Companies: Its top 3 holdings, Banco Santander Chile (BSAC), Banco de Chile (BCH), and Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) collectively account for around 35% of the ETF's assets. Banks began to break out during the second half of 2023, while Chile's main lithium mining company pulled back strongly in 2023 due to weak industry sentiment.

Chile's banks are still attractively valued and offer a high dividend yield, while the overall ETF offers a much lower yield. These banking companies can be a solid alternative for dividend investors who also want exposure to the economy.

Industry Info: Other notable standouts about this ETF include the fact that it is heavily invested in consumer names (circa 26% of assets) yet still has a very low valuation.

Some of the other cheaper regional markets like Colombia and Brazil trade at single digit PE ratios, but partially because of the industry concentration.

Colombia: Colombia's MSCI Index primarily consists of banking stocks and also invests in other industries like materials and utilities.

Brazil: Brazil, which trades at 11x earnings, also has a higher weighting in areas like banking and materials, and lower weighting in consumer themes.

This is not to write off Brazil and Colombia, which look like some of the most stellar value plays in emerging markets, but to note that Chile's stock market discount is significant both to regional peers, emerging markets, and its historical average.

Risks and Final Thoughts

Election in 2025: Chile will hold its elections in November 2025, and the markets will likely be very volatile before and after this event, perhaps similar to some of the 2019 selloffs. There have also been some protests in recent months.

One factor to note is that Chile's stock market has been very resilient amid multiple political setbacks, specifically since November 2019 when there were widespread protests in the country. Historically, Chile has been able to bounce back from these risks in the long run, but short-term pain is likely inevitable.

This ETF is only down 6% since these conflicts, including two rejections of a new proposed constitution in 2022 and 2023.

Trade War: The biggest threat approaching is the upcoming trade war, which sent US equities tumbling on Wednesday. Emerging markets, including Chile, could sell off in April, which will create a nice entry point for Chilean equities because they are already undervalued. Chile's top export destinations are the United States and China, so it will likely be very volatile in the upcoming months before the country's elections. While US markets tumbled on Wednesday, this ETF opened in the green. Chile is not a primary target of trade war tensions, but could be indirectly impacted later this year.

The biggest risk with this ETF at the moment is that there are many short-term threats that could result in pullbacks between April to November. If you are hesitant about emerging markets or want to keep more cash on the sidelines, this makes sense. However, Chile is widely underrepresented in many emerging market indexes and has a lot of hidden potential that many investors overlook. It absolutely makes sense to be overweight in Chile in this environment and to continue holding if you want emerging market exposure.

data.title: Top 50 High-Quality Dividend Growth Stocks For April 2025
data.description:
High-Quality Dividend Stock Investable Universe

On September 1, 2024, I started tracking an investable universe of what I believe to be 50 high-quality dividend growth stocks. You can find out more about the formation of this investable universe in the original article.

The objective of this investable universe is to present me with opportune investment ideas at any given point in time. As share prices swing up and down and business fundamentals change daily, my custom tracking tool updates the valuation ratings for each of these stocks and helps me figure out which ones I should focus my attention on.

The investable universe, as a whole, got off to a good start this year, but many of the individual components did not go unscathed in the recent market sell-off. The average loss for all 50 included stocks in March was 4.02%, compared to a loss of 5.86% for the SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY) and a loss of 1.16% for Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Here are the year-to-date returns:

Investable Universe: -0.92%

SPY: -4.56%

SCHD: +3.26%

Below is an update for this investable universe as of April 2, 2025.

Investable Universe

Here is a snapshot of the full investable universe including share price as of 4/2/25, current valuation rating, forward rate of return estimate, the valuation rating from 2/28/25 and whether or not it has changed.

My investment objective is simple, I want to identify "High Quality" businesses and I want to buy them at a fair or better price. I use this investable universe, along with the valuation model, to narrow my focus.

12 out of the 50 stocks in my universe had their valuation rating change since 2/28/25. 8 of those changes were favorable while the other 4 were downgrades.

In last month's update, we dove deeper into the 9 stocks that all had a favorable valuation rating and at least a 10% expected rate of return. This month we will look at the 8 stocks with the highest expected rate of return.

Monolithic Power Systems

Final Thoughts

To reiterate, the objective of this investable universe is to help me identify which stocks may be opportune investments at a given point in time. While all 50 of these stocks are potentially high-quality businesses, it makes more sense to focus on the companies that have stronger track records, appear reasonably or attractively valued and present a desirable future rate of return.

data.title: Voya Corporate Leaders Trust Fund B Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

A passively managed grantor trust that invests in most of the same 30 blue-chip companies bought in 1935 or their direct descendants.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump's presidential victory, with the S&P 500 Index (SPX)(SP500) rising by 2.41% and the Nasdaq Composite advancing by 6.17%. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation and the U.S. Federal Reserve's more conservative rate-cut path. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index declined by 3.06% and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

An investor should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund(s) carefully before investing. For a free copy of the Fund's prospectus or summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, visit us at www.voyainvestments.com or call (800) 992-0180. Please read all materials carefully before investing.

Portfolio review

The underperformance during the quarter was driven primarily by selection effects. Stock selection effects within energy, industrials and financials sectors were the main detractors for performance. Relative overweights to industrials and energy were also major detractors. Key individual detractors included overweights in Union Pacific Corp. (UNP), Marathon Petroleum Corp. (MPC) and Linde PLC (LIN).

Stock selection effects in the utilities sector was a contributor. Not allocating to the health care sector contributed. Key individual contributors for the period included, not owning Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY) and UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH). As of the end of the reporting period the Strategy's largest sector overweights included the industrials and energy sectors, and to a lesser extent materials. The largest underweight sectors were information technology, consumer discretionary and health care. The Trust does not currently hold positions within the information technology or real estate sectors. Sector exposures are purely a function of the strategy's quantitative investment discipline, however, and are not actively managed.

Outlook

After months of noise surrounding the U.S. presidential election, markets have now refocused on macroeconomic data, which offer mixed signals. Key concerns include global geopolitical tensions, especially around tariffs, and deteriorating sentiment tied to mega-cap positioning and broader market weakness. Despite these challenges, U.S. equities should continue to benefit from robust consumer spending, optimism around artificial intelligence and solid corporate earnings. U.S. inflation is projected at 2.5% for 2025, but the new administration's policies could reverse the disinflation trend.

Holdings Details:

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of Portfolio investments, as of 12/31/24: Union Pacific Corp. 38.50%, Marathon Petroleum Corp. 10.88%, Linde PLC. 9.38%, Microsoft Corp. 0.00%, Eli Lilly and Co. 0.00% and UnitedHealth Group Inc. 0.00%. 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to change on a daily basis.

The Standard and Poor's 500 Index is designed to measure performance of the broad domestic economy through changes in the aggregate market value of 500 stocks representing all major industries. The index includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available market capitalization. Index returns do not reflect fees, brokerage commissions, taxes or other expenses of investing. Investors cannot invest directly in an index.

All investing involves risks of fluctuating prices and the uncertainties of rates of return and yield inherent in investing. Investing in funds that are concentrated in a smaller number of holdings poses greater risk than investing in funds with a larger number of holdings because each investment has a greater effect on performance. The value of a participation fluctuates with the market value of the underlying portfolio securities of the Trust. The dividend income, if any, from the portfolio securities is subject to fluctuation which in turn will affect the amounts of distributions made to participants. An investor in the Trust has no assurance against loss in a declining market, and redemption at a time when the market value of the participations is less than their cost will result in a loss to the investor.

Taxation: For Federal income tax purposes, (1) the Trust will be treated as a fixed investment trust and will not be subject to Federal income tax, (2) each participant will be treated as the owner of their pro rata portion of the common stock of the corporations held by the Trust, (3) each participant will be required to include in their gross income their pro rata portion of the dividends and interest received by the Trust (including the amounts of such dividends and interest that are not distributed to participants but are used to pay the fees and expenses of the Trust), at the time such dividends and interest are received by the Trust, not at the later time such dividends and interests are distributed to participants or reinvested in additional participations, and (4) for taxable years beginning before 2026, each noncorporate (including individual) participant will generally not be permitted to deduct their pro rata portion of the fees and expenses of the Trust. Please see the prospectus for further information. The Trust is a long-term investment. Prior to investing, investors should consider their ability to pursue investment in successive trusts, if available. Investors should also be advised that the sponsor may terminate the Trust, which is a unit investment trust, earlier than the specified termination date of the Trust.

data.title: STRF: New Perpetual Shares From Strategy (MicroStrategy), 10% Yield
data.description:
Thesis

Strategy (MSTR), formerly known as MicroStrategy, has just issued its first perpetual preferred equity, in the form of Series A 10% Preferred Equity (NASDAQ:STRF). The securities are Nasdaq listed and issued in a registered format under the Securities Act of 1933. The preferred shares offer a high 10% coupon and are cumulative.

In today's article we are going to explore this innovative new listing, benchmark its risk and rewards versus the common shares and convertible securities, and highlight the risks faced by investors in the securities.

What has been issued?

(Micro)Strategy has announced the issuance of five million shares of perpetual preferred stock:

TYSONS CORNER, Va.—(BUSINESS WIRE)—March 18, 2025—Strategy™ (Nasdaq: MSTR; STRK) today announced that, subject to market and other conditions, it intends to offer, in a public offering registered under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended (the “Securities Act”), 5,000,000 shares of Strategy’s Series A Perpetual Preferred Stock (the “perpetual strife preferred stock”).

One can find the full prospectus here, but below please find a summary of the most important analytical aspects regarding the shares:

  • Ticker: STRF (Nasdaq listing)
  • Par Value: $100/share
  • Interest Rate: 10%
  • Maturity Date: Perpetual
  • Dividend type: Cumulative
  • Payment Dates: March, June, September, December
  • Compound Dividend: Yes (10% + 100 bps)
  • Maximum compound dividend rate: 18%

The company has the option to pay a cash dividend (the dividend is only in cash), and if it skips the cash payment then the dividend accrues on the due amount, akin to a pay-in-kind instrument. The interest rates move up yearly if the cumulative interest rate is not settled:

In the event that any accumulated regular dividend on the perpetual strife preferred stock is not paid on the applicable regular dividend payment date, then additional regular dividends (“compounded dividends”) will accumulate on the amount of such unpaid regular dividend, compounded quarterly. The compounded dividend rate applicable to any unpaid regular dividend that was due on a regular dividend payment date will initially be a rate per annum equal to 10% plus 100 basis points; provided, however, that, until such regular dividend, together with compounded dividends thereon, is paid in full, such compounded dividend rate will increase by 100 basis points per annum for each subsequent regular dividend period, up to a maximum dividend rate of 18% per annum.

This feature basically represents a protection mechanism for investors who are afraid the company might choose to defer all interest payments. Via these punitive rates the prospectus makes Strategy pay higher rates if it defers interest payments.

The best way to think about STRF

STRF represents an issuance of 'classic' preferred shares. They are perpetual, and senior only to the common equity. Kindly keep in mind that MSTR has a whole slew of convertible notes outstanding, notes which are senior to the preferred shares and have in some instances mandatory convertible features:

For the purposes of investment, assume a 10-year duration for STRF while in reality do expect these preferred shares to be outstanding for the foreseeable future or until bitcoin does a parabolic up-move (think +50% or +100% from here), which would allow MSTR to issue new ones at a cheaper rate. We view STRF as a permanent form of capital.

While they have no defined maturity date, STRF is senior to the common shares and sports a large coupon, which should make this class of security much less volatile versus the common shares.

Comparison versus common shares and converts

The MSTR common shares are a leveraged play on bitcoin. As bitcoin goes up, the MSTR shares increase in value, as seen in the past few years:

Similarly, if bitcoin prices crash, the common shares will see a substantial mark-to-market loss.

The convertible securities issued by Strategy (as outlined in the table above), have a low to zero coupon, and are a play on the upside in bitcoin. The securities represent senior notes with maturity dates (i.e. they are senior unsecured debt for the company) and will have upside in case bitcoin soars and thus moves the MSTR common shares significantly up.

Conversely, the preferred shares represent a quasi-permanent form of capital, and in the case bitcoin soars (i.e. doubling from current levels), one should expect the preferred shares to stay close to par value since Strategy might be able to re-finance them cheaper. STRF is therefore a classic fixed income instrument with a perpetual maturity date (i.e. a high duration). The securities are best suited for income oriented investors who believe Strategy is here to stay (i.e. it will not go bankrupt) and that bitcoin is an asset class which holds future potential. A range bound market for bitcoin (think $50,000 to $100,000) is ideal for STRF holders since it would not introduce too much downside volatility while keeping the probability of re-finance in check.

Like any fixed income instrument, STRF will have duration risk and credit spread risk. We are going to discuss these risks further in the 'Risk Factors' section, but basically STRF will incur a forecasted -10% loss if 10-year rates move higher by 100 bps from here, and similarly a -10% loss if bitcoin crashes and the market prices the MSTR probability of default as present in credit spreads wider by 100 bps. Kindly keep in mind that STRF are the first preferred shares linked to a bitcoin company, and thus represent new pricing territory for all market participants.

Another item to note is that the issuance of innovative convertible and preferred shares is making Strategy inextricably linked with the overall bitcoin ecosystem, thus increasing its long-term survival probability.

Risk Factors and Upside

The largest risk factor here by far is the price of bitcoin. If bitcoin really crashes (think prices below $20,000), then the market will assign MSTR a higher probability of default. As per its own disclosures, MSTR's average bitcoin purchase price is roughly $66,000:

As a leveraged strategy, the more bitcoin falls below that level, the higher the probability of default. A high probability of default translates into wider credit spreads, so one can theoretically see a -30% drawdown in STRF if the market requires 14% current yields on the securities. We computed some numbers assuming a 10-year duration:

Also to note the price of bitcoin is asymmetric here. If bitcoin goes to $200,000, the preferred shares have very little upside because the market will think they will be refinanced for a cheaper rate. Unlike the converts, there is no embedded upside optionality here.

The second-largest risk factor here is represented by rates. If rates move up significantly, STRF will lose value. In our assumed 10-year duration profile, a 100 bps increase in long rates will result in a -10% drawdown.

STRF is a fixed income instrument with little upside. The securities represent a way to clip a coupon if Strategy is solvent. As discussed above, via its structure, STRF does not stand to gain if bitcoin moves up in value.

Conclusion

STRF represents exchange listed preferred shares from Strategy. The shares have been issued with a 10% cumulative coupon, and have pay in kind features to prevent the company from skipping cash payments. The securities have very little upside, and are an asymmetric play on bitcoin. While STRF is set to lose value if bitcoin crashes, it will trade close to par in an up-bitcoin scenario due to the probability of the company re-financing the shares for cheaper. The shares represent a less volatile way to speculate on Strategy's solvency and they do best in a range bound bitcoin scenario

data.title: NXP Semiconductors: Prepare For More Pain In The Coming Months
data.description:
Risk and uncertainty are beginning to play a major role in equity markets in 2025 and with that higher risk and more cyclical areas of the market are likely to suffer.

Unfortunately, there are more bad news for NXP Semiconductors N.V. (NASDAQ:NXPI) shareholders. The stock is already down by more than 18% over the past year and it appears that 2025 will be yet another challenging period.

As we could see on the graph above, the recent downward movement was not driven by broader market movements which resulted in the stock's beta falling significantly. However, that does not mean that NXPI is all of a sudden a low market risk stock, quite the contrary.

Moreover, the uptick in trading volume in recent months is another red flag that investors are losing confidence in the stock.

All that is a clear indication that investors should not rush and buy the stock, even in the face of what looks like a relatively low multiple for a business operating in the highly attractive semiconductors space.

Even though NXPI might look attractive when we take into account the stock's recent history, the rest of 2025 is likely to remain challenging, and I would abstain from imitating a position.

More Uncertainty

As a starting point, NXPI positioning within the semiconductors space puts the company at a disadvantage to some peers.

Even though long-term growth in the automotive and industrial segments is still expected to remain in double digits on an annual basis, these areas of the market are more heavily influenced by the business cycle. On the contrary, investments in Generative AI are less likely to be reduced in the face of a potential recession or a global trade war.

Within these two areas of operation, NXPI's main growth driver is expected to be the automotive sector and, more specifically - newly emerging areas of accelerated growth (shown in light blue on the graph below).

These accelerated growth areas comprise of software-defined vehicles, radar systems, electrification and vehicle connectivity. All of these are heavily influenced by demand of electric vehicles and high-end models - two areas of the automotive market that are likely to suffer the most in an event of an economic slowdown.

So far, the good news for NXPI is the company's significant exposure to the booming automotive sector in China, which could remain largely resilient on the back of increasing market share of Chinese OEMs.

The major problem of NXPI, however, stems from the fragility of the complex supply chains in the automotive sector and the potential shift towards local manufacturing on the back of the raging trade war.

The latest 25% tariff on automobile imports in the U.S. threatens to disrupt the current supply chains and could have long-term repercussions on the sector. On top of that, vehicle prices are likely to increase, which will take its toll on future demand for NXPI's chips.

As a result of all that, demand visibility is worsening, which is bad news for investors and NXPI's management when it comes to planned expansion efforts and inventory management.

Bleak Outlook For 2025

While risk and uncertainty around NXPI's business have increased significantly in recent months, the stock's lower price does not seem attractive enough to justify a buy rating.

On a historical basis, NXPI's price/sales multiple used to reflect the company's current margins until 2022. Since 2022, NXPI appears to be trading at a discount to what its current gross margins would suggest.

However, as I outlined in late 2023, these margin improvements did not seem sustainable over the long-run which was one of the main reasons why I have retained a hold rating on the stock.

Even though the latter appears as an unlikely scenario at this point in time, the record high operating margins appear unsustainable as supply chains normalize and competition continues to intensify.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Since then, things have not improved with NXPI's revenue now expected to remain flat over the coming years.

The lower revenue was blamed for NXPI's sharp fall in gross profitability during the last reported quarter which is foreshadowing what's at stake in 2025, if demand does not recover.

The uncertain demand environment is also increasing inventory risk for NXPI as its management is trying to keep the channel inventories under control. So far, some inventory digestion is anticipated in 2025, but this largely depends on-demand resiliency and the recent increase in Days inventory outstanding (DIO) and consequent worsening of the cash conversion cycle could have sustained negative implications on both margins and free cash flow.

The recently provided guidance for Q1 2025 did little to dispel any fears of lower-than-anticipated demand and with that, there's little hope that NXPI could deliver satisfactory shareholder returns in the near future.

Conclusion

As NXPI continues its descent, the stock does not appear more attractive. Macroeconomic uncertainty will most likely continue to weigh on returns in 2025 and more profitability headwinds are also to be expected. Low visibility and the disappointing Q1 2025 guidance make matters worse, and the probability of NXPI delivering satisfactory returns in the coming months is quite low.

data.title: HCA Healthcare: Attractive Even With Medicaid Risk
data.description:
Shares of HCA Healthcare (NYSE:HCA) have had a bumpy ride over the past year, while still managing a 5% gain. Republicans in Congress are ramping up their work on a budget reconciliation bill to cut taxes, and as part of this package, there may be Medicaid cuts, which could hit hospital revenue. I last covered shares in December, rating them a “buy,” and since then, they have added 16%. Given potential policy changes, now is a good time to revisit HCA. I remain bullish.

Looking first to Medicaid issues that have driven much of the headlines lately, I would note that as part of the continuing resolution passed in March, congress extended several key healthcare provisions, including providing funding for individual hospitals that provide more Medicaid care. By maintaining most healthcare-related policies that otherwise would have expired, Congress avoided making cuts to the sector, essentially allowing it to continue to run on a current policy baseline. Still, bigger changes may be coming

House Republicans are looking to potentially cut Medicaid by $880 billion over a decade, though this figure has received some pushback in the Senate. For context in fiscal 2024, $618 billion of grants were sent to states for Medicaid. To achieve this spending cut, federal Medicaid spending would need to be cut by 12-14%. Now, federal spending is only about 70% of total spending, so if states held their spending steady, total Medicaid funding would decline by about 8%.

Of course, we do not know for sure how states would react, as some could seek to increase their spending to offset federal cuts, but others could attempt to roll back eligibility alongside the Federal government. Additionally, cuts may not necessarily be proportional; hospitals could face a harsher or more lenient outcome.

Given the moderating impact of the Senate, I would expect an 8% funding cut to be the worst case scenario and would view a 5% impact as a more reasonable baseline. This cut would likely be due to a combination of lower reimbursement and tighter eligibility. As such, we could see some existing Medicaid users move onto a subsidized managed care insurance offering. I would expect this impact to be fairly modest. In aggregate, I would expect Medicaid-related revenue to have a ~3-5% headwind from legislation. This impact would likely begin to be felt in late 2025 and more fully in 2026.

This is why payer-mix is so important. HCA has a relatively modest exposure to Medicaid at about 11% of revenue. As such, the total impact to the company would likely be less than 0.5% of revenue or about $350 million, from a combination of fewer services provided and lower payments on the services it does provide. Assuming a 50% incremental margin, this is a ~$0.50 headwind to long-run EPS, or about a 2% risk to earnings. It is also worth noting that HCA already loses money on its Medicaid customers, so the ability to cut hospital payments significantly without jeopardizing rural care in particular is unclear.

There is no disputing that potential Medicaid changes are a headwind for HCA and justify a lower share price, all else equal, but the ultimate impact is likely to be relatively modest. This impact is certainly not large enough to offset all of the positives we are seeing from the company in my view.

Recapping recent results, in the company’s fourth quarter reported back on January 24th, HCA earned $6.22, which beat estimates by $0.08. Demand remains robust with 3% growth in same facility admissions. HCA also continues to recover pricing after facing a margin squeeze post-COVID. Inpatient revenue per admission rose by 4.4% to $19,800. We have also seen a favorable mix shift as inpatient surgeries rose by 2.8% while outpatient surgeries declined by 1.3%.

Thanks to these pricing gains and a normalizing labor market, margins are improving. Most importantly, salaries and benefits declined by 0.4% to 43.3% of revenue, though supply costs increased by 0.4% to 15.3%. With the labor market continuing to soften and utilization remaining strong, I expect to see some modest further decrease in salary expense as a share of revenue.

HCA remains a highly cash generative business, and it generated about $5.6 billion in free cash flow last year. It is directing all of this cash flow back to its investors. HCA primarily returns capital to investors via buybacks. Shares have a very modest 0.8% dividend yield, even after HCA increased the payout by 9% in January. In contrast, it did $6 billion of 2024 buybacks, reducing its share count by about 6%.

HCA has been able to return capital in excess of cash flow because of its strong balance sheet. It has $1.9 billion of cash and $43 billion of debt. Relative to its $13.9 billion of EBITDA, that leaves it with 3x debt/EBITDA, at the low end of its 3-4x target. Last quarter, it actually reduced its leverage target to 2.75-3.75x, which is prudent in a higher interest rate environment.

Of course, as HCA grows EBITDA, it can carry proportionately more debt while maintaining the same leverage profile. I expect it to grow EBITDA at least $800 million in 2025, providing another ~$2.5 billion of debt capacity. As such, I expect at least $8 billion of capital returns in 2025. Indeed, HCA has a new $10 billion authorization, and management aims to complete “a significant portion” in 2025. Its guided share count implies $6-8 billion of buybacks this year.

Looking at guidance more broadly. Management expects 5% revenue growth and about 7% EBITDA growth. Back in December, I was targeting at least $24-$24.50 in 2025 earnings power. Guidance points to higher EPS, partly because its share count is falling a bit more quickly than I anticipated, as buybacks may be front-half loaded.

I do not expect material intra-2025 Medicaid impacts, though with inflation slowing, I see revenue coming in at or a bit below the mid-point, leading to EPS of $24.50-$25.25.

When valuing shares of HCA, I think investors should factor in the fact that Medicaid is likely to be a headwind starting in 2026 of about $0.50, given my base case that Republicans can pass a budget bill. As such, I view run-rate earnings as closer to $24-$24.75, with HCA essentially having a $0.50 benefit from higher Medicaid this year that is unlikely to recur.

That leaves shares with a ~14x multiple and a 7% free cash flow (factoring in $150 million of Medicaid cash flow headwinds). I view this attractively, as HCA can return all free cash flow to investors and is well positioned to grow cash flow at least 3% per year, given the country’s aging population and ~2+% inflation. That means, even at $350, HCA can deliver long-term 10% returns.

It appears that shares are factoring in Medicaid headwinds, and I would remain a buyer. If Congress does not cut Medicaid, I see another $7-10 of upside. Over the next year, I see shares pushing towards $390, giving ongoing share count reduction and modest earnings growth, making HCA a buy, especially given its relatively low sensitivity to economic uncertainty.

data.title: The Trade War Trapdoor
data.description:
Stocks crashed today for no apparent reason. Try as they might, analysts couldn’t pinpoint a catalyst, then stumbled upon a trade policy proclamation from the White House yesterday. That might have been it.

Level Change 4/3/25 (%)

-4.0 Dow

-6.0 Nasdaq

-5.4 Nasdaq 100

-4.8 S&P 500

-6.7 S&P 400

-7.1 S&P 600

Indeed, the worst crash since covid was all about President Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariff announcement landing heavier than expected. Making the 1930 Smoot-Hawley effort look like a bake sale, he raised America’s tariff wall by sevenfold, to an average import duty of nearly 23%, up from a quaint 3%.

According to the World Trade Organization, here’s the new landscape, before retaliatory fun begins:

Average Tariff Rates (%)

23 USA

12 India

8.4 South Korea

6.7 Brazil

6.3 Thailand

6.1 Saudi Arabia

6.0 Chile

5.3 Russia

5.1 Vietnam

3.9 Mexico

3.4 Canada

3.3 UK

3.0 China

2.7 EU

1.9 Japan

The VIX volatility gauge took one look at that and vaulted like a biotech stock with a rumor and no revenue, surging 40% from 21.5 to 30.

Wall Street’s dunk tank was set extra deep. The S&P took its worst drenching since June 2020 when second-wave covid fears spiked sanitizer sales. The Dow notched its fourth-biggest decline this decade. The Nasdaq fell its most since March 2020, when the Fed cut interest rates to near zero, confirming there was something to be nervous about.

Small-cap stocks officially entered bear-market territory, with the S&P 600 now down 21.9% from its November 25 peak, the very month when now-Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent penned his Wall Street Journal valentine to “Trump’s Economics.”

In it, he credited the post-election rally to “expectations of higher growth, lower volatility and inflation, and a revitalized economy for all Americans.” From last Sunday’s Kelly Letter: “Nothing crashes harder than high hopes…”

Retaliation threats are stacking up faster than disclaimers in a crypto prospectus.

China vowed a stiff response unless the tariffs are lifted. Canada, keeping it neighborly, will match the 25% auto tariffs tit-for-tat with an equal levy on American vehicles. The EU is calibrating a response. France’s prime minister called the tariffs “a catastrophe for the economic world.” From a country that’s handled downturns with decapitation, that’s saying something.

Mexico, however, twirled into a hat dance after being spared the rod, one of the few free-trade partners to dodge it. Avocado toast is safe, and really, isn’t that what counts?

The Trump administration remains serenely convinced that the duties' dragnet will yield lasting economic bounty. Commerce Secretary Lutnick assured CNN viewers that Trump will not back down from this “reordering of global trade.” The president himself told reporters, “The markets are going to boom.”

Bears replied, “Oh, there was a boom, all right.” They doubled down on stagflation warnings, brushing off the idea that recent declines have priced in the damage. No, they say, 2025 earnings estimates show valuations have plenty of altitude left to lose. Beatings will continue until ratios improve.

Fedspeak played along. Vice Chair Philip Jefferson detected uncertainty in the air and craves more data. Fed Governor Lisa Cook foresees lower growth and higher inflation, citing “tariffs and other policy changes.”

Let the trade negotiations begin, before the damage requires a stimulus package and a cold compress.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
